claim: SWE-bench became the industry standard for evaluating coding agents not because of its design but because of Devin's (Cognition) high-profile use of it
actionable: When choosing evaluation frameworks, consider adoption by leaders in the field as a signal of benchmark quality, not just theoretical merits
source_title: [State of Code Evals] After SWE-bench, Code Clash & SOTA Coding Benchmarks recap — John Yang

claim: Unit tests as verification have limitations; long-running code clash tournaments where agents maintain codebases and iterate over rounds better capture real development
actionable: For comprehensive agent evaluation, use multi-round benchmarks where agents iterate on shared codebases rather than single-instance unit test verification
source_title: [State of Code Evals] After SWE-bench, Code Clash & SOTA Coding Benchmarks recap — John Yang

claim: SWE-bench variants (Pro, Live, Multilingual) are expanding the benchmark space, with newer variants justifying themselves through curation techniques rather than just 'more repos'
actionable: When creating eval variants, justify them through explicit curation techniques (language diversity, repo characteristics) rather than just increasing dataset size
source_title: [State of Code Evals] After SWE-bench, Code Clash & SOTA Coding Benchmarks recap — John Yang

claim: Impossible tasks in benchmarks (Tau-bench) are intentionally included to detect cheating—any model claiming high scores on impossible tasks is likely gaming the benchmark
actionable: Include intentionally impossible tasks in your evaluation benchmarks as a cheating detector; any model reporting success on them should raise red flags
source_title: [State of Code Evals] After SWE-bench, Code Clash & SOTA Coding Benchmarks recap — John Yang

claim: There's tension between long-autonomy agents (5+ hour runs) and interactive development; real developer workflows emphasize fast iteration loops, not long unsupervised runs
actionable: When evaluating agents, consider both long-run autonomy benchmarks and interactive feedback loop benchmarks, as real development uses both patterns
source_title: [State of Code Evals] After SWE-bench, Code Clash & SOTA Coding Benchmarks recap — John Yang

claim: Terminal-bench unlocked creativity by letting non-CS PhD students create programming competition environments, showing benchmarks benefit from diverse perspective input
actionable: When designing benchmarks, involve people from diverse backgrounds and non-coders to create more creative and realistic evaluation scenarios
source_title: [State of Code Evals] After SWE-bench, Code Clash & SOTA Coding Benchmarks recap — John Yang

claim: Future code evals should evaluate economically valuable arenas and measure real business impact rather than just code correctness in isolation
actionable: Design agent evaluation tasks around real business outcomes (revenue, speed, quality) rather than academic correctness metrics
source_title: [State of Code Evals] After SWE-bench, Code Clash & SOTA Coding Benchmarks recap — John Yang

claim: Benchmark authors should invest in building realistic user simulators to evaluate agent performance under realistic interaction patterns, not just predefined task sequences
actionable: When building benchmarks, invest in creating realistic user simulators that can provide dynamic feedback to agents rather than static test cases
source_title: [State of Code Evals] After SWE-bench, Code Clash & SOTA Coding Benchmarks recap — John Yang
